title: Static-Field Tunneling Ionization in Space-Fractional Quantum Mechanics
abstract: Tunneling ionization in static or slowly varying electric fields is a cornerstone of strong-field physics and provides the entry point for semiclassical descriptions of above-threshold ionization and high-harmonic generation. In conventional quantum mechanics, the Perelomov--Popov--Terent'ev (PPT) theory and its Ammosov--Delone--Krainov (ADK) form yield an ionization rate whose defining feature is an exponential dependence governed by an under-barrier (imaginary-time) action. Here we develop an analytical ADK-like tunneling model within \emph{space-fractional} quantum mechanics, where the quadratic kinetic energy is replaced by the Riesz fractional Laplacian of order $1<α\le2$. Working in a static electric field in the length gauge, we derive a closed-form tunneling exponent for a triangular exit barrier. The fractional kinetic operator deforms the conventional $I_p^{3/2}$ scaling to $I_p^{1+1/α}$ and introduces a characteristic $\sin(π/α)$ factor encoding the complex-phase structure associated with nonlocal dispersion. We position this benchmark relative to prior tunneling studies in fractional quantum mechanics (primarily scattering through model barriers and fractal potentials) and provide a validation protocol for testing the exponent in time-dependent simulations of the fractional Schrödinger equation under a constant field. The result establishes a transparent reference for static-field ionization in nonlocal quantum dynamics and a baseline for strong-field approaches extensions.
summary: This paper develops an analytical model for tunneling ionization in space-fractional quantum mechanics. It shows that non-local kinetic energy changes the scaling laws of ionization and significantly enhances the ionization rate compared to standard quantum mechanics. The work provides a new benchmark for studying how non-locality affects strong-field physics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of a standard strong-field benchmark for tunneling ionization in Space-Fractional Quantum Mechanics (SFQM) and seeks to understand how non-local kinetic energy affects the tunneling exponent.
main_result: The author derives a closed-form fractional-ADK (fADK) tunneling exponent and demonstrates that decreasing the fractional order alpha enhances ionization rates due to genuine non-local transport effects.
method: The study uses a semiclassical WKB approach to derive the analytical exponent and performs numerical simulations of the 1D time-dependent fractional Schrödinger equation using a split-operator scheme.
model_or_system: A 1D model of an electron in a static electric field with a soft-core Coulomb potential, governed by a fractional Hamiltonian featuring the Riesz fractional Laplacian of order 1 < alpha <= 2.
key_observables: Tunneling ionization rate (Gamma), tunneling exponent, survival probability, and the tunneling action (slope of -ln Gamma vs 1/F0).
important_parameters: Fractional order (alpha), ionization potential (Ip), static electric field strength (F0), and the barrier-suppression threshold.
main_assumptions: The analytical derivation assumes a triangular exit barrier; the model is restricted to one dimension; and the study focuses on the static-field (quasistatic) limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares tunneling rates for different alpha values; Figure 4 benchmarks the 1D-TDSE against standard ADK theory; Figure 5 shows scaling for various alpha values under a fixed potential; Figure 6 compares the numerical 1D-TDFSE results against the derived fADK prediction.
paper_structure: The paper reviews conventional ADK/PPT theory, introduces the fractional Hamiltonian framework, provides the analytical derivation of the fADK exponent, presents numerical validation protocols, and concludes with a comparison of numerical results against the new theory.
why_interesting: It provides a new theoretical benchmark for non-local quantum dynamics and explores how non-quadratic kinetic energy fundamentally alters the tunneling mechanism, which is relevant to anyone studying non-local transport or fractional dynamics in atomic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison of −ln Γ(1/F0) for the conventional ADK model (α = 2.0) and fractional-like tunneling models with α = 1.5 and α = 1.1. Here we set Ip = −0.67 a.u.

FIG. 3. Ground-state probability density |ψ0(x)|2 for α = 1.1, 1.5, and 2.0 (semilogarithmic scale), illustrating the progres- sive delocalization and heavier spatial tails induced by de- creasing the fractional order α.
FIG. 2. Ground state energy E0 as a function of the fractional parameter α, for Z = 1 and a = 1.

FIG. 4. Benchmark of static field tunneling ionization in the standard case (α = 2). (a) Negative logarithm of the ioniza- tion rate, −ln Γ, as a function of the inverse field strength 1/F0, comparing 1D-TDSE results (symbols) with the an- alytical ADK tunneling prediction (solid line). (b) Ratio ΓTDSE/ΓADK shown on a logarithmic scale. The ratio re- mains of order unity with only weak field dependence over the field range F0 = 0.04–0.07 a.u., demonstrating that the DC propagation and rate-extraction procedure reliably repro- duce standard tunneling behavior.

Figure 4(a) shows the negative logarithm of the ioniza- tion rate, −ln Γ as a function of the inverse field strength 1/F0 over the range F0 = 0.04−0.07 a.u. The TDSE data (symbols) follow an approximately linear dependence on 1/F0, consistent with the exponential tunneling scaling predicted by ADK theory (solid line). A single mul- tiplicative factor is used to align the ADK curve with the numerical data at a reference field of F0 = 0.05 a.u, reflecting the known difference between one-dimensional model calculations and the three-dimensional ADK pref- actor, while preserving the field dependence.
